original_abstract: This volume examines educational inequalities among the children of Turkish immigrants in Austria, France, and Sweden. Despite being one of the largest immigrant populations in these countries, Turkish families frequently contend with discrimination and restricted pathways to advancement—challenges that are clearly reflected in their children’s educational experiences. Drawing on extensive cross-national data, this study explores how different school systems and family dynamics shape the academic trajectories of the second generation. A primary finding is that national education systems offering more flexible institutional arrangements—such as late tracking and higher permeability—significantly foster greater upward social and economic mobility. By highlighting the interaction between state policy and individual resources, this research provides critical insights into why some integration contexts are more successful than others in bridging the opportunity gap.
humanized_abstract: Children of Turkish Immigrants in Denmark, Germany, the Netherlands and Sweden Educational Aspirations and their Relation to Educational Expectations, School Performance and Social and Economic Positions This study investigates educational inequalities among the second generation of Turkish immigrants in four North European countries — Austria, France, Sweden — that have one of the largest numbers of immigrant descendents. In many respects their parents experience discrimination, face barriers in the labour market and have difficult conditions to lead a fulfilling life for themselves and their families — conditions that are more than reflected in the conditions for their children at school. This study, grounded in a rich cross-national data set, adds to the literature by documenting how families cope with these constraints, and how the schools respond to these challenges within institutional frameworks that affect the opportunity structures for social mobility for the second generation. It is found that all the countries in the study in which there is more institutional flexibility — late tracking and more permeable school structures — have better social and economic positions for immigrant descendents. This study is therefore a study on conditionality and effect relations concerning policy and individual capacities. It helps us understand the conditions for effective different integration strategies and can thus comment on the relative performance of different countries in their efforts to bridge the opportunity gap between native populations and disadvantaged immigrant groups.
